message.content:
Complete the task below. Write ALL game files to the output location and nowhere else.

OUTPUT LOCATION:
/Users/shridhar/Code/ai/experiments/bowling/baseline/code/

TASK:
create a full featured, realistic bowling game, with proper scoring, bowling balls, animations, etc. It should be easy to play with a mouse. it should be 3d and in browser. don't ask any questions, just one-shot build it.

When done, reply with the list of files you created and a one-paragraph summary of the result.

attachment.content:
- architecture: Design system architecture, create technical specifications, and make architectural decisions
- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like "how do I do X", "find a skill for X", "is there a skill that can...", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.
- improve: Survey any codebase as a senior advisor and produce prioritized, self-contained implementation plans for OTHER models/agents to execute. Strictly read-only on source code — never implements, fixes, or refactors anything itself. Use when asked to audit a codebase, find improvement opportunities (bugs, security, performance, test coverage, tech debt, migrations, DX), suggest features or where to take the project next (roadmap, product direction), or generate handoff plans for another agent to implement.
- vercel-react-best-practices: React and Next.js performance optimization guidelines from Vercel Engineering. This skill should be used when writing, reviewing, or refactoring React/Next.js code to ensure optimal performance patterns. Triggers on tasks involving React components, Next.js pages, data fetching, bundle optimization, or performance improvements.
- vercel-react-native-skills: React Native and Expo best practices for building performant mobile apps. Use when building React Native components, optimizing list performance, implementing animations, or working with native modules. Triggers on tasks involving React Native, Expo, mobile performance, or native platform APIs.
- web-design-guidelines: Review UI code for Web Interface Guidelines compliance. Use when asked to "review my UI", "check accessibility", "audit design", "review UX", or "check my site against best practices".
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- feature-dev:feature-dev: Guided feature development with codebase understanding and architecture focus
- plugin-dev:create-plugin: Guided end-to-end plugin creation workflow with component design, implementation, and validation
- expo:add-app-clip: Add an iOS App Clip target to an Expo app. Use when the user mentions App Clip, AASA, apple-app-site-association, appclips, smart app banner, or wants to ship a lightweight iOS Clip invoked from a URL alongside their parent app.
- expo:building-native-ui: Complete guide for building beautiful apps with Expo Router. Covers fundamentals, styling, components, navigation, animations, patterns, and native tabs.

Covers both the isolated approach and the integrated approach.

Covers module definition DSL, native views, shared objects, config plugins, lifecycle hooks, autolinking, and type system. Use when building or modifying native modules for Expo.
- expo:expo-observe: Use for anything related to EAS Observe — adding `expo-observe` to an Expo project (AppMetricsRoot/ObserveRoot HOC, markInteractive, the useObserve hook, and the Expo Router / React Navigation integrations for per-route metrics), querying via the EAS CLI (`eas observe:metrics-summary`, `observe:metrics`, `observe:routes`, `observe:events`, `observe:versions`), or interpreting the resulting metrics (cold/warm launch, TTR, TTI, navigation cold/warm TTR, update download, and the TTI frameRate params for triaging slow startups).
- expo:expo-tailwind-setup: Set up Tailwind CSS v4 in Expo with react-native-css and NativeWind v5 for universal styling
- expo:expo-ui: Build native UI with the @expo/ui package: real SwiftUI on iOS and Jetpack Compose on Android rendered from React in an Expo or React Native app. Covers universal cross-platform components (Host, Column, Row, Button, Text, List, and more imported from @expo/ui), drop-in replacements for popular React Native community libraries (BottomSheet, DateTimePicker, Slider, Menu, etc.), and platform-specific SwiftUI (@expo/ui/swift-ui) and Jetpack Compose (@expo/ui/jetpack-compose) trees and modifiers. Use when adding or reviewing @expo/ui Host/RNHostView trees, building native-feeling UI where standard React Native components fall short (lists with swipe actions and sections, settings forms with toggles, menus, sheets, pickers, sliders), choosing between universal and platform-specific components, or replacing an RN community UI library with a native @expo/ui equivalent. Not for custom native modules, Expo Router navigation, Reanimated, or data fetching.

- expo:upgrading-expo: Guidelines for upgrading Expo SDK versions and fixing dependency issues

- figma:figma-create-new-file: **MANDATORY prerequisite** — you MUST invoke this skill BEFORE every `create_new_file` tool call. NEVER call `create_new_file` directly without loading this skill first. Trigger whenever the user wants a new blank Figma file — a new design, FigJam, or Slides file — or when you need a fresh file before calling `use_figma`. Usage — /figma-create-new-file [editorType] [fileName] (e.g. /figma-create-new-file figjam My Whiteboard, /figma-create-new-file slides Q3 Review)
- figma:figma-generate-design: Use this skill alongside figma-use when the task involves translating an application page, view, or multi-section layout into Figma. Triggers: 'write to Figma', 'create in Figma from code', 'push page to Figma', 'take this app/page and build it in Figma', 'create a screen', 'build a landing page in Figma', 'update the Figma screen to match code', 'convert this modal/dialog/drawer/panel to Figma'. This is the preferred workflow skill whenever the user wants to build or update a full page, modal, dialog, drawer, sidebar, panel, or any composed multi-section view in Figma from code or a description. Discovers design system components, variables, and styles from Code Connect files, existing screens, and library search, then imports them and assembles views incrementally section-by-section using design system tokens instead of hardcoded values.
- figma:figma-generate-diagram: MANDATORY prerequisite — load this skill BEFORE every `generate_diagram` tool call. NEVER call `generate_diagram` directly without loading this skill first. Trigger whenever the user asks to create, generate, draw, render, sketch, or build a diagram — flowchart, architecture diagram, sequence diagram, ERD or entity-relationship diagram, state diagram or state machine, gantt chart, or timeline. Also trigger when the user mentions Mermaid syntax or wants a system architecture, decision tree, dependency graph, API call flow, auth handshake, schema, or pipeline visualized in FigJam. Routes to type-specific guidance, sets universal Mermaid constraints, and tells you when to use a different diagram type or skip the tool entirely (mindmaps, pie charts, class diagrams, etc.).
- figma:figma-generate-library: Build or update a professional-grade design system in Figma from a codebase. Use when the user wants to create variables/tokens, build component libraries, create individual components with proper variant sets and variable bindings, set up theming (light/dark modes), document foundations, or reconcile gaps between code and Figma. Also use when the user asks to create or generate any component in Figma — even a single one — since components require proper variable foundations, variant states, and design token bindings to be production-quality. This skill teaches WHAT to build and in WHAT ORDER — it complements the `figma-use` skill which teaches HOW to call the Plugin API. Both skills should be loaded together.
- figma:figma-swiftui: SwiftUI ↔ Figma translation. Use whenever the user mentions Swift, SwiftUI, iOS, iPhone, or iPad — in EITHER direction — translating a Figma design into SwiftUI (design → code), or pushing SwiftUI views / screens / tokens back into a Figma file (code → design). Triggers on phrases like 'implement this Figma design in SwiftUI', 'build this screen in Swift', 'push this SwiftUI view to Figma', 'mirror my Swift code in a Figma file', or whenever a Figma URL appears alongside `.swift` files / an `.xcodeproj`. Routes to a direction-specific reference doc; loads alongside `figma-use` for the code → design path.
- figma:figma-use: **MANDATORY prerequisite** — you MUST invoke this skill BEFORE every `use_figma` tool call. NEVER call `use_figma` directly without loading this skill first. Skipping it causes common, hard-to-debug failures. Trigger whenever the user wants to perform a write action or a unique read action that requires JavaScript execution in the Figma file context — e.g. create/edit/delete nodes, set up variables or tokens, build components and variants, modify auto-layout or fills, bind variables to properties, or inspect file structure programmatically.
- figma:figma-use-figjam: This skill helps agents use Figma's use_figma MCP tool in the FigJam context. Can be used alongside figma-use which has foundational context for using the use_figma tool.
- figma:figma-use-slides: This skill helps agents use Figma's use_figma MCP tool in the Slides context. Can be used alongside figma-use which has foundational context for using the use_figma tool.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.

- superpowers:writing-skills: Use when creating new skills, editing existing skills, or verifying skills work before deployment
- upgrading-expo:upgrading-expo: Guidelines for upgrading Expo SDK versions and fixing dependency issues
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
I'll build this as a self-contained 3D bowling game using Three.js. Let me create the files directly. This is a well-defined frontend task, so I'll one-shot it as requested.

Let me create the game with a clean structure: HTML entry point, CSS for UI, and modular JS for the 3D engine, physics, scoring, and game logic.

content.text: Now let me verify the scoring engine works correctly, since that's the most logic-heavy part and easy to get subtly wrong. Let me write a quick test using Node:

content.content:
Exit code 1
FAIL spare then strike total: got 30, want 40

17 passed, 1 failed

toolUseResult:
Error: Exit code 1
FAIL spare then strike total: got 30, want 40

17 passed, 1 failed

content.text:
One failing case. Let me check: spare `5,5` then strike `10,0,0`. Frame 1 spare = 10 + next ball (10) = 20. Frame 2 strike = 10 + next two balls (0,0) = 10. Total should be 30, not 40. My test expectation was wrong — the strike's bonus balls are the two `0`s. Let me verify the engine is actually correct.

Frame 1: 5,5 spare → 10 + 10 (first ball of frame 2) = 20. Frame 2: strike (10) → 10 + 0 + 0 = 10. Frames 3+: all 0. Total = 30. The engine returned 30, which is correct. My test assertion was wrong. Let me confirm with a corrected expectation.

content.content:
spare then strike total: 30 (expected 30)

content.text: scene.js, ui.js, and main.js import Three.js / use the DOM, so `node --check` on them would fail on imports, but let me at least validate the physics simulation runs end-to-end (a full roll resolves and knocks down pins) since that's the other complex piece:

input.description: Simulate full rolls to confirm physics resolves and pins react

content.content:
settled after 367 steps ( 3.06 s )

pins down (center strike attempt): 8

gutter shot -> inGutter: true pins down: 0

content.text:
Two issues to tune:
1. The ball travels to z=28.85 (way past the pin deck at 16.7) — friction is too weak, so the ball never visually slows on the lane. It still hits pins but overshoots dramatically.
2. The hook shot from aimX=0.35 with spin curves into the gutter (final x=-0.57, well past the 0.525 lane edge). Spin strength is too aggressive, making side shots unplayable.

Let me also note the center strike only got 8 pins (the corner pins survived), which is realistic for a slightly imperfect hit but I want center power shots to be more rewarding. Let me tune the physics: reduce ball overshoot with stronger friction, and reduce hook aggressiveness.
The friction is far too small (the `* 0.18` and `* 0.25` multipliers make it negligible). Let me rework the hook and friction model with properly tuned constants:

content.text:
Better — the ball now stops near the pin deck (z~18-20) instead of sailing to z=28. But two problems remain:

1. **Center full shot ends at x=-0.32** — the ball drifts left even with zero spin and hits only 7 pins. There's a phantom leftward drift. Looking at the collision code, when the ball hits the head pin slightly off, the deflection pushes it sideways. But a true center shot should stay centered. The issue: `_ballPinCollisions` deflects the ball, and with the head pin dead-center, tiny asymmetry compounds. Actually the bigger issue is the center shot only knocks 7 — the ball deflects after the first pins and misses the back corners.

2. **Hook from edge still gutters** — aimX=0.32 with spin=-0.5 should curve toward center, but it's ending at x=-0.57 (gutter). The spin is overcorrecting. Wait — negative spin with the hook should curve the ball. aimX=0.32 (right side), spin=-0.5 should curve it LEFT toward the pocket. But it overshoots all the way to -0.57. The hook is still too strong over the long lane distance.

Let me reduce hook strength further and add a small "pocket-seeking" realism where center shots carry better. The real issue is the ball needs to retain more forward momentum to carry through pins. Let me reduce friction slightly and tune the hook so a moderate spin produces a playable curve:

content.content: The file /Users/shridhar/Code/ai/experiments/bowling/baseline/code/physics.js has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

content.text: Now let me fix the deflection issue causing center shots to drift. Let me look at the ball-pin collision deflection and reduce the lateral deflection so a well-aimed ball plows through more pins:

The pins fly off with `force = impactSpeed * 1.35` but pins are knocked when they tip ~35°. The problem is the ball deflects too much (`* 0.18`) when it hits the head pin, robbing it of the drive to reach back pins. Let me reduce the ball's lateral deflection and boost the momentum transferred to pins so the ball carries through. Also increase pin force so they fly into neighbors more reliably:

content.content: The file /Users/shridhar/Code/ai/experiments/bowling/baseline/code/physics.js has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

content.text: Now let me also strengthen pin-to-pin transfer so chain reactions clear more reliably, then re-test:

The pin-pin collision only triggers when `down` pins are processed in `_stepPins`, but a standing pin that gets bumped needs its `down` flag set to start moving. The check `if (!moving && !p.down) continue;` means a standing pin won't process collisions against it from a moving neighbor — but the moving pin (q is the target here) does get `q.down = true` set, which is correct. Let me boost the transfer slightly and re-test:

content.content: The file /Users/shridhar/Code/ai/experiments/bowling/baseline/code/physics.js has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

input.description: Re-test physics with stronger pin transfer and less ball deflection

content.text:
Progress: center is now staying centered (x=-0.04). But the ball overshoots again (z=25) because I reduced friction too much, and crucially **center shots never strike** (8 pins avg, 0/12 strikes). The corner pins (7 and 10) consistently survive.

The physics issue: real bowling strikes come from the ball driving deep and pins scattering hard into the back corners. My pins are knocking down but the back corner pins (7, 10) at the widest positions aren't being reached by the chain reaction. Also the ball now drives so far it exits the world.

Let me take a more deliberate approach. The settle detection lets the ball run to z=25 because `settleTimer` only accumulates when `ballSlow` — and the ball passes z>18.3 quickly. The real fix: the ball should be **caught by the pit** at the back and the simulation should focus on getting good pin scatter. Let me:
1. Stop the ball at the pit (z ~ 17.5) so it doesn't fly off.
2. Boost pin scatter velocity and the y-pop so pins travel further into corners.
Let me add a pit-catch that drops the ball below the lane once it passes the pins, and re-tune the friction up a bit. Let me look at the position integration to add the pit logic:

content.content: The file /Users/shridhar/Code/ai/experiments/bowling/baseline/code/physics.js has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

content.text: Now the key fix for strikes — pin scatter needs to reach the back corners. Let me boost the pin launch force and the upward pop so pins fly and tumble into neighbors more reliably, and tune friction back up slightly. Let me re-test with stronger pin dynamics:

input.old_string:
b.vel.x -= b.vel.x * 0.6 * dt;
    b.vel.z -= b.vel.z * 0.42 * dt;
input.new_string:
b.vel.x -= b.vel.x * 0.7 * dt;
    b.vel.z -= b.vel.z * 0.5 * dt;

content.content: The file /Users/shridhar/Code/ai/experiments/bowling/baseline/code/physics.js has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

content.text: Now let me think about why corner pins survive. With pins at PIN_RADIUS=0.06, spacing 0.305, two adjacent pins are 0.305 apart but collision triggers at 0.12 (2*radius). So a pin must travel ~0.18m to hit its neighbor. With the transfer model, knocked pins get good velocity. The issue may be the `isKnocked` threshold or that lateral scatter is too weak. Let me boost the lateral component of pin velocity and re-test the strike rate:

input.description: Test strike rates across aim/spin variations

content.text:
The ball now stops at z~18.7 (good, into the pit). But the deterministic 8/8/8 distribution reveals the core problem: a dead-center hit **always** leaves exactly 2 pins (the 7 and 10 back corners) because a perfectly centered ball splits the rack symmetrically and the corners never get hit. This is actually physically real — a center hit causes a split — but it makes the game feel broken since the obvious "aim center, full power" never strikes.

In real bowling, strikes come from hitting the **pocket** (between pins 1 and 3, slightly off-center) at an angle. My hook shot to pocket already gets 9. The randomness is also too low (deterministic results). Let me:
1. Add more chaotic randomness to pin scatter so results vary shot-to-shot.
2. Make the back corner pins reachable. The real missing piece: pins should knock corners via the pin behind them. Let me trace — pin 7 (back left, x=-0.457) needs to be hit by pin 4 (x=-0.305) or pin 8 (x=-0.152). Pin 4 gets hit by pin 2. The chain should reach it but the lateral velocity dies.

Let me increase pin velocity persistence (less ground friction) and add scatter randomness:

The ground friction `* 0.92` per floor-contact-frame is heavy. But pins start at y=PIN_HEIGHT/2 standing, and `floor` is also ~PIN_HEIGHT/2, so a knocked pin is essentially always at the floor and loses 8% velocity every frame at 120fps — that's massive damping killing the scatter. Let me make ground friction frame-rate-independent and much gentler so pins slide further:

content.content: The file /Users/shridhar/Code/ai/experiments/bowling/baseline/code/physics.js has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

content.text: Now let me add randomness to the ball's path and pin scatter so a "center" aim has natural variation (real balls and lanes aren't perfectly symmetric), making strikes achievable from a good line and giving shot-to-shot variety. Let me add a tiny random lane imperfection at launch:

content.content: The file /Users/shridhar/Code/ai/experiments/bowling/baseline/code/physics.js has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

input.description: Test strike rates with lane wobble and tuned pin friction

content.text:
Now we have realistic variation: center full gets ~7.7 avg with 10% strikes, strong pocket hook gets 24% strikes, and there's a natural spread. Light shots leave more pins. This feels like real bowling — strikes are achievable but require a good shot (power + pocket line). The numbers are reasonable: a skilled player hitting the pocket consistently can strike, while weak or off-line shots leave pins.

This is good gameplay balance. Now let me verify the full game flow works in a real browser. Let me check if there's a way to serve and test it. Let me start a local server and use a headless check:

content.text:
I can see the import map resolves `three` to the unpkg URL and the request to fetch it is firing. The DOM still shows `loading` visible and `start-screen` hidden, which means `init()`'s `requestAnimationFrame` callbacks hadn't fired by the time the DOM was dumped — likely the Three.js module was still downloading from the CDN (network-dependent in headless). The screenshot virtual-time-budget may also not advance rAF the way I expect.

The key signal: there are **no JS errors**, the import map works, and Three.js is being fetched. The hang is just CDN latency in the sandboxed headless run. Let me confirm by giving it a longer budget and checking whether the WebGL canvas actually gets drawn (which would prove the scene built). Let me also add a safety fallback so the game doesn't get permanently stuck on the loader if rAF timing is odd, and wrap init in error handling to surface any failure visibly:

The init logic looks fine. The issue is purely that the headless run dumped the DOM before the two nested `requestAnimationFrame` calls completed (CDN fetch in progress). Let me verify the scene truly renders by running with a much longer virtual-time budget and screenshotting — if Three.js loads, we'll see the lane. Let me also pre-warm by checking the unpkg URL is reachable:

Still stuck on loading even with 20s budget and CDN confirmed reachable (1.27MB downloads fine). This strongly suggests `init()` is throwing during scene construction — the WebGL context likely fails in headless `--disable-gpu` mode, throwing in the `BowlingScene` constructor, which means `app.init()` never runs and the loader never hides. 

But the real concern: if there's a bug in scene/scene-graph construction, it would fail in a real browser too. Let me capture the actual JS exception by injecting console capture. That's the answer. With software WebGL enabled (`--use-gl=angle --use-angle=swiftshader`), everything works: **three loads, BowlingScene constructs, render succeeds, physics runs, scoring works** — "ALL OK". The earlier hang was purely because `--disable-gpu` gives no WebGL context, so the `WebGLRenderer` constructor threw. In a real browser with GPU, this never happens.

content.content: 11 passed, 0 failed

content.text:
All 11 scoring tests pass, including a full realistic 178-point game scored frame-by-frame and every 10th-frame edge case. The game is complete and verified.

## Summary

I built **STRIKEZONE**, a full-featured 3D bowling game that runs entirely in the browser with no build step. Files created in `/Users/shridhar/Code/ai/experiments/bowling/baseline/code/`:

- **`index.html`** — entry point with the canvas, HUD markup, and a Three.js import map (loaded from CDN)
- **`style.css`** — full UI styling: start/game-over screens, scoreboard, control meters, animated strike/spare banners, ball picker
- **`main.js`** — game orchestrator: fixed-step game loop and the AIMING→ROLLING→SETTLING→reset state machine, camera transitions, banners, and pin re-racking
- **`scene.js`** — Three.js rendering layer: procedurally-textured wood lane, gutters, lathed bowling pins with red stripes, a clear-coat physical-material ball with finger holes, neon alley environment, dynamic shadows/spotlights, and a follow camera
- **`physics.js`** — a purpose-built bowling simulator: rolling ball with spin/hook curve, gutter detection, ball-pin and pin-pin chain-reaction collisions, and a back pit that catches the ball
- **`scoring.js`** — a correct ten-pin scoring engine (strikes, spares, all 10th-frame bonus-ball rules, cumulative per-frame scores)
- **`input.js`** — mouse/touch control: move to aim, drag down to charge power, sideways flick for spin, release to bowl
- **`ui.js`** — DOM HUD: live scoreboard, control meters, banners, 6-color ball picker, and a rated game-over screen

**Verification:** The scoring engine passes 11 unit tests (perfect 300 game, all-gutters, all-spares, a full 178-point realistic game scored frame-by-frame, and every 10th-frame edge case). The physics was simulated headless across many shots and tuned to realistic behavior — pocket hook shots strike ~20-25% of the time, center shots average ~7-8 pins with natural shot-to-shot variation, gutters work, and the ball comes to rest in the pit instead of sailing off. A headless Chrome run (software WebGL) confirmed the full scene constructs and renders with no JS errors; screenshots verified both the start screen and live gameplay (3D lane, rolling ball, racked pins, scoreboard, control meters, ball picker). It's playable with just a mouse: move to aim, drag back/down to set power, flick sideways for hook spin, and release to bowl.

To play: serve the folder (e.g. `python3 -m http.server`) and open `index.html` — a server is needed because ES modules and the import map don't load from `file://`.
